category.text: $course$/CSIR NET Mathematics/Similar Questions/Part B/Linear Algebra — Diagonalizability

questiontext.text: An \(n\times n\) matrix \(A\) is diagonalizable (over \(\mathbb{C}\)) if and only if:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B) For every eigenvalue, its geometric multiplicity equals its algebraic multiplicity

Diagonalizability \(\Leftrightarrow\) there is a basis of eigenvectors \(\Leftrightarrow\) for each eigenvalue \(\lambda\), \(\dim\ker(A-\lambda I)=\) algebraic multiplicity of \(\lambda\). Equivalently, the minimal polynomial has no repeated roots.

answer.text: For every eigenvalue, geometric multiplicity = algebraic multiplicity

questiontext.text: Which matrix is NOT diagonalizable over \(\mathbb{R}\)?

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (D) \(\begin{pmatrix}1&1\\0&1\end{pmatrix}\)

\(\begin{pmatrix}1&1\\0&1\end{pmatrix}\) has eigenvalue \(\lambda=1\) with algebraic multiplicity 2 but geometric multiplicity 1 (only one independent eigenvector \((1,0)^T\)). It is a Jordan block and is NOT diagonalizable. The other matrices have distinct eigenvalues (hence diagonalizable).

questiontext.text: If an \(n\times n\) matrix has \(n\) distinct eigenvalues, then it is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A) Diagonalizable

Eigenvectors corresponding to distinct eigenvalues are linearly independent. If all \(n\) eigenvalues are distinct, there are \(n\) linearly independent eigenvectors forming a basis. Hence \(A\) is diagonalizable. The converse is false: \(\begin{pmatrix}1&0\\0&1\end{pmatrix}\) is diagonalizable with repeated eigenvalue 1.

questiontext.text: Every complex square matrix is similar to a matrix in Jordan normal form. The Jordan block for eigenvalue \(\lambda\) of size \(k\) is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (C) An upper triangular matrix with \(\lambda\) on the diagonal and 1's on the superdiagonal

A Jordan block \(J_k(\lambda)\) has \(\lambda\) on the diagonal, 1 on the superdiagonal, and 0 elsewhere. It represents a nilpotent perturbation: \(J_k(\lambda)=\lambda I+N\) where \(N\) is the nilpotent Jordan block. Every matrix is similar to a block diagonal of Jordan blocks (Jordan decomposition).

answer.text: A lower triangular matrix with \(\lambda\) on the diagonal
An upper triangular matrix with \(\lambda\) on the diagonal and 1's on the superdiagonal
A matrix with \(\lambda^k\) in the \((1,k)\) entry

questiontext.text: Every real symmetric matrix \(A=A^T\) is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B) Orthogonally diagonalizable: \(A=Q\Lambda Q^T\) with \(Q\) orthogonal

Real spectral theorem: every real symmetric matrix is orthogonally diagonalizable. \(A=Q\Lambda Q^T\) where \(\Lambda\) is diagonal (real eigenvalues) and \(Q\) is orthogonal (eigenvectors as columns). This is the foundation of PCA, quadratic forms, and many areas of applied mathematics.

answer.text: Diagonalizable but eigenvectors need not be orthogonal
Diagonalizable only if all eigenvalues are distinct

questiontext.text: If \(A=PDP^{-1}\) with \(D=\begin{pmatrix}2&0\\0&3\end{pmatrix}\), then \(A^5\) equals:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (C) \(P\begin{pmatrix}32&0\\0&243\end{pmatrix}P^{-1}\)

\(A^5=(PDP^{-1})^5=PD^5P^{-1}\). \(D^5=\begin{pmatrix}2^5&0\\0&3^5\end{pmatrix}=\begin{pmatrix}32&0\\0&243\end{pmatrix}\). This is why diagonalization is valuable: matrix powers are trivial in diagonal form.

questiontext.text: A matrix \(A\) is diagonalizable if and only if its minimal polynomial:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A) Has no repeated roots (is a product of distinct linear factors)

Diagonalizable \(\Leftrightarrow\) minimal polynomial splits into distinct linear factors \(\Leftrightarrow\) minimal polynomial = \(\prod(\lambda-\lambda_i)\) with all \(\lambda_i\) distinct. If the minimal polynomial has a repeated root, the corresponding Jordan block has size \(\geq2\), preventing diagonalization.

answer.text: Has degree equal to the rank of \(A\)

questiontext.text: The matrix \(A=\begin{pmatrix}2&0&0\\0&2&0\\0&0&3\end{pmatrix}\) is diagonalizable because:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (C) It is already diagonal, so it trivially satisfies the diagonalizability criterion

Any diagonal matrix is diagonalizable (it equals \(I\cdot A\cdot I^{-1}=A\)). The eigenvalue 2 has algebraic multiplicity 2 and geometric multiplicity 2 (the eigenspace is the \(xy\)-plane, a 2-dimensional subspace). Since geometric = algebraic for all eigenvalues, \(A\) is diagonalizable.

answer.text: It is already diagonal; the geometric multiplicity of eigenvalue 2 equals its algebraic multiplicity (both = 2)

questiontext.text: If \(A\) and \(B\) are diagonalizable matrices with \(AB=BA\), then:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B) \(A\) and \(B\) are simultaneously diagonalizable

Commuting diagonalizable matrices are simultaneously diagonalizable — there exists a single basis of vectors that are eigenvectors for both \(A\) and \(B\). This is a key theorem used in quantum mechanics (commuting observables have common eigenstates).

answer.text: \(AB\) is diagonalizable but \(A\) and \(B\) individually may not be

questiontext.text: The rotation matrix \(R=\begin{pmatrix}0&-1\\1&0\end{pmatrix}\) (rotation by \(90°\)) is NOT diagonalizable over \(\mathbb{R}\) because:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (D) Its characteristic polynomial \(\lambda^2+1\) has no real roots

\(\det(\lambda I-R)=\lambda^2+1\), which has no real roots (\(\lambda=\pm i\)). Since complex eigenvalues come in conjugate pairs for real matrices, and there are no real eigenvalues, \(R\) has no real eigenvectors and is not diagonalizable over \(\mathbb{R}\). Over \(\mathbb{C}\), it diagonalizes to \(\begin{pmatrix}i&0\\0&-i\end{pmatrix}\).

answer.text: Its characteristic polynomial \(\lambda^2+1\) has no real roots

questiontext.text: For \(A=\begin{pmatrix}3&1&0\\0&3&0\\0&0&2\end{pmatrix}\), is \(A\) diagonalizable?

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B) No; the eigenspace for \(\lambda=3\) has dimension 1 but algebraic multiplicity 2

Eigenvalues: \(\lambda=3\) (algebraic mult. 2) and \(\lambda=2\) (mult. 1). For \(\lambda=3\): \(\ker(A-3I)=\ker\begin{pmatrix}0&1&0\\0&0&0\\0&0&-1\end{pmatrix}\). The solution has only one free variable: \(x_1\) free, \(x_2=0\), \(x_3=0\). So geometric mult.=1 \(<\) algebraic mult.=2. NOT diagonalizable.

answer.text: No; the eigenspace for \(\lambda=3\) has dim 1 but algebraic mult. 2
No; all upper triangular matrices are non-diagonalizable

questiontext.text: Eigenvectors corresponding to distinct eigenvalues of a matrix are:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (C) Linearly independent

Standard theorem: if \(v_1,\ldots,v_k\) are eigenvectors corresponding to distinct eigenvalues \(\lambda_1,\ldots,\lambda_k\), they are linearly independent. Proof by induction using the assumption that a dependence relation leads to a contradiction with the distinctness of eigenvalues.

questiontext.text: The eigenvalues of a real skew-symmetric matrix (\(A^T=-A\)) are:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B) Purely imaginary or zero

If \(Av=\lambda v\) (over \(\mathbb{C}\)), then \(\lambda\|v\|^2=\langle Av,v\rangle=-\langle v,Av\rangle=-\bar\lambda\|v\|^2\), so \(\lambda=-\bar\lambda\), meaning \(\lambda\) is purely imaginary (or zero). A \(2\times2\) example: \(\begin{pmatrix}0&-1\\1&0\end{pmatrix}\) has eigenvalues \(\pm i\).

name.text: Diagonalization - Diagonalizable iff Minimal Poly has No Repeated Factor

questiontext.text: The matrix \(A=\begin{pmatrix}1&0\\0&1\end{pmatrix}\) has minimal polynomial:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A) \(\lambda-1\)

\(A=I\), so \(A-I=0\). The minimal polynomial of the identity matrix is \(\lambda-1\) (the smallest degree polynomial making \(A-I=0\)). The characteristic polynomial is \((\lambda-1)^2\). Since the minimal polynomial has no repeated roots (degree 1, root \(\lambda=1\) once), \(A\) is diagonalizable — trivially, since it's already diagonal.

questiontext.text: If \(A^2=I\) (involution), then \(A\) is diagonalizable with eigenvalues:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (C) \(\pm1\) only, and \(A\) is diagonalizable

The minimal polynomial of \(A\) divides \(\lambda^2-1=(\lambda-1)(\lambda+1)\), which has no repeated roots. So \(A\) is diagonalizable with eigenvalues in \(\{1,-1\}\). The eigenspaces are \(\ker(A-I)\) and \(\ker(A+I)\), and \(\mathbb{R}^n=\ker(A-I)\oplus\ker(A+I)\).

questiontext.text: Among all \(2\times2\) real matrices with characteristic polynomial \((\lambda-1)^2\), those that are diagonalizable are:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A) Only \(I=\begin{pmatrix}1&0\\0&1\end{pmatrix}\)

With char. poly \((\lambda-1)^2\), the only eigenvalue is 1. Diagonalizable \(\Leftrightarrow\) geometric mult.=2 \(\Leftrightarrow\) \(A-I=0\) \(\Leftrightarrow\) \(A=I\). Any other matrix with this characteristic polynomial is of the form \(\begin{pmatrix}1&a\\0&1\end{pmatrix}\) (a Jordan block) with geometric mult.=1.

answer.text: All upper triangular matrices with 1 on the diagonal

questiontext.text: Let \(A\) be a real symmetric positive definite matrix. Then \(A\) can be written as \(A=B^TB\) where \(B\) is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B) An invertible upper triangular matrix (Cholesky decomposition)

Cholesky decomposition: every real symmetric positive definite matrix \(A\) factors as \(A=L L^T\) (lower triangular \(L\)) or equivalently \(A=R^TR\) (upper triangular \(R\)). This is used in numerical linear algebra for efficient computation.

answer.text: An invertible upper triangular matrix (Cholesky decomposition)

questiontext.text: Two diagonalizable matrices \(A\) and \(B\) are simultaneously diagonalizable if and only if:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (C) \(AB=BA\)

Two diagonalizable matrices are simultaneously diagonalizable iff they commute. This generalises to families of matrices: a set of commuting diagonalizable matrices is simultaneously diagonalizable. The "only if" direction is clear: diagonal matrices commute; the "if" direction requires more work.

questiontext.text: Every \(n\times n\) matrix over \(\mathbb{C}\) is similar to an upper triangular matrix (Schur triangularization). This implies:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A) Every complex matrix has at least one eigenvalue

Since \(\mathbb{C}\) is algebraically closed, the characteristic polynomial always has a root. More: Schur's theorem says \(A=QUQ^*\) with \(Q\) unitary and \(U\) upper triangular. The diagonal entries of \(U\) are the eigenvalues. This shows every complex matrix has \(n\) eigenvalues (counting multiplicity).

answer.text: Every complex matrix has at least one eigenvalue
Every real matrix is diagonalizable over \(\mathbb{R}\)

name.text: Diagonalization - CSIR Style: Which is NOT Diagonalizable

questiontext.text: Which \(2\times2\) real matrix is NOT diagonalizable over \(\mathbb{R}\)?

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (C) \(\begin{pmatrix}0&-1\\1&0\end{pmatrix}\)

Characteristic polynomial: \(\lambda^2+1\) — no real roots. Not diagonalizable over \(\mathbb{R}\). Option (A) \(\begin{pmatrix}1&2\\2&1\end{pmatrix}\): eigenvalues \(-1,3\) (distinct) — diagonalizable. Option (B) \(\begin{pmatrix}2&0\\0&2\end{pmatrix}=2I\) — diagonalizable. Option (D) \(\begin{pmatrix}1&1\\0&2\end{pmatrix}\): eigenvalues 1, 2 (distinct) — diagonalizable.
